description: This figure presents transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization results for Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel following solution treatment at 1000°C for 1 h and aging at 550°C for 3 h. The composite image comprises high-resolution TEM micrographs and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) elemental mapping, as reported in the caption, to visualize precipitate morphologies and elemental distributions. The micrographs reveal nanoscale precipitates including the Cu-rich phase exhibiting irregular oval morphology approximately 10 nm in diameter and bar-shaped variants roughly 30 nm in length, alongside dot-like or bar-shaped κ-carbide precipitates, both embedded in the austenitic matrix. The EDS maps demonstrate clear elemental partitioning where κ-carbide regions are enriched in Fe, Mn, and Al but depleted in Cu, whereas Cu-rich phase regions show significant Cu enrichment with corresponding depletion of Fe, Mn, and Al. Selected area electron diffraction patterns or lattice fringe analysis indicate a cube-on-cube orientation relationship with coherent interfaces between austenite, κ-carbide, and Cu-rich phase. This figure supports the co-precipitation mechanism where κ-carbide precipitation precedes Cu-rich phase formation, with Cu atoms rejected from κ-carbide enriching the surrounding matrix to enable subsequent Cu-rich phase nucleation.

This figure presents high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) imaging and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns of co-precipitates in aged Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu (3Cu) austenitic low-density steel. The main HRTEM image displays lattice fringes at the nanometer scale, distinguished by two marked regions: a yellow dashed box labeled γ+κ representing the austenitic matrix containing κ-carbide precipitates, and a red dashed box labeled γ+Cu indicating the austenitic matrix with Cu-rich phase precipitates. The insets provide corresponding SAED patterns: the yellow-bordered inset shows the diffraction pattern for the γ+κ region along the [011] zone axis with indexed spots including (011) and (111), while the red-bordered inset presents the SAED pattern for the γ+Cu region along the [011] zone axis with spots indexed as (111) and (200). According to the caption and citation text, the Cu-rich phase exhibits BCC structure in this specific analysis (though subsequent text suggests FCC), and critically, no additional diffraction spots are observed beyond those from the Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide, confirming both precipitates maintain coherence with the austenitic matrix. The patterns support a cube-on-cube orientation relationship of [110]γ//[110]κ//[110]Cu and (111)γ//(111)κ//(111)Cu, with scale bars indicating 5 nm for the HRTEM image and 2 1/nm for the SAED patterns.

This figure presents comprehensive transmission electron microscopy characterization of aged 3Cu steel (Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu) to elucidate the co-precipitation mechanism of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide. Panel (a) shows a low-magnification TEM image (scale bar 50 nm) revealing numerous nanoscale precipitates distributed in the austenitic matrix after solution treatment at 1000°C followed by aging at 550°C for 3 hours. Panel (b) displays EDS elemental mapping of Fe, Mn, Al, and Cu, where caption states the white dashed box indicates κ-carbide enriched in Fe, Mn, and Al but depleted in Cu, while the magenta-lined region shows Cu-rich phase with concentrated Cu and depleted matrix elements. Panels (c) and (d) provide high-resolution TEM images (scale bar 5 nm) showing Cu-rich phase morphologies: (c) exhibits an irregular oval shape approximately 10 nm in diameter, while (d) shows bar-shaped precipitates approximately 30 nm in length with the inset revealing lattice fringes spacing of 0.182 nm. The figure supports the core conclusion that κ-carbide precipitation expels Cu atoms into the surrounding austenitic matrix, creating local Cu enrichment that subsequently precipitates as coherent Cu-rich phases, thereby establishing a mutual promotion mechanism between the two nanoscale precipitates.
